summary: In this mailbag-style post, Joel Spolsky responds to reader questions covering computer science education, software licensing, the tech job market during the dot-com crash, bootstrapping a software company, and version control tools. He shares candid details about Fog Creek's finances, revealing that FogBUGZ covers non-salary expenses while they bootstrap toward profitability with CityDesk. He advises a new grad to take a New York banking job and wait for better opportunities, and counsels an aspiring entrepreneur on the realities of building a product company from consulting revenue. The post offers a transparent window into the early-stage struggles and strategy of a small software company during the 2001 recession.
key_points: Computer science education should emphasize practical skills like database design, UI/UX, writing functional specs, debugging, and especially writing — because great programmers are great communicators.
During the 2001 dot-com crash, interesting R&D software jobs in New York City were scarce, but Microsoft offered valuable learning opportunities despite its suburban location.

Fog Creek's bootstrapping strategy involved using consulting revenue to fund product development, shipping FogBUGZ as a low-risk first product because they already had it, and conserving marketing spend for their bigger bet, CityDesk.
FogBUGZ succeeded in a crowded market of 1000 competitors by focusing on simplicity ('painless bug tracking') and fanatical customer service where callers spoke directly to programmers.
Joel recommends CVS over Visual SourceSafe, citing SourceSafe's poor reputation for data reliability.
money_quotes: Every local programmer I know complains all the time about how hard it is to get interesting software jobs that aren't at a bank.
The good thing about working for Microsoft is that Microsoft is one company that really knows how to make software, and you'll learn a lot that will help you wherever you go.
I think in your place I would come to the city and spend some time hopping around, waiting for something interesting to show up. Pay your dues at the banks and media companies like the rest of us!

It has more than paid for its own R&D costs, so now it feels like one of those mystical things you hear about on late-night TV that you set up and get a nice check every month in exchange for doing nothing.
There are about 1000 bug tracking packages on the market (from companies who are probably selling it for the same reason as we are — because they have it.)

People are also happy because we're a small company that provides fanatical customer service… when you find a bug and call our toll-free number, you'll speak to a programmer and probably get a bug fix shipped to you that day.

We hope to be able to demonstrate that x dollars spent on marketing results in x times y dollars in revenue. At this point, if y > 1, we can make a strong argument to raise money for marketing without giving up very much equity.
key_insight: A bootstrapped software company can succeed even in a crowded market and a recession by shipping what it already has, providing exceptional customer service, and patiently reinvesting revenue rather than chasing outside funding.
